title: Magnifica Humanitas — Introduction and Chapter 1: Babel vs Jerusalem
publisher: Dicastery for Promoting Integral Human Development
original_filename: Infographic 1 - Magnifica Humanitas - Introd & Cap 1 - EN B.jpg
summary: Two roads ahead: Babel (humanity without God, uniformity, dehumanisation, technology as instrument of dominance) vs Jerusalem (God at the centre, diversity preserved, brotherhood and collaboration, technology as instrument of service). The Tower of Babel Syndrome diagnosed against Nehemia's way of rebuilding the city wall, with every family assigned its own section. Four pillars for building a better future emerge as the constructive frame for the encyclical.
bridges: Bridge 4: The institution as carrier of human flourishing
Bridge 6: Limits on what may be commodified

title: Magnifica Humanitas — Chapter 2: Foundations and Principles of Social Doctrine
publisher: Dicastery for Promoting Integral Human Development
summary: The structural foundations of Catholic Social Doctrine reaffirmed and extended: the dignity of the human person as imago Dei, the common good, the universal destination of goods, subsidiarity, solidarity, and the preferential option for the poor. The infographic gathers these principles into a single architecture and ties them back to the Rerum Novarum-to-present arc that the encyclical inherits and updates for the AI age.
bridges: Bridge 2: The moral architecture of property
Bridge 3: Commutative justice and the just price
Bridge 4: The institution as carrier of human flourishing

title: Magnifica Humanitas — Chapter 3: Technology and Dominance / AI
publisher: Dicastery for Promoting Integral Human Development
summary: Technology as a site of moral discernment: artificial intelligence as the contemporary Babel-temptation when treated as an instrument of dominance, surveillance, or replacement of the human; AI as a tool of service when integrated under human judgment, ordered to the common good, and constrained by the dignity of the worker. The chapter distinguishes legitimate development from the totalising claim that technology resolves the human question on its own.
bridges: Bridge 5: The dignity of commercial work
Bridge 6: Limits on what may be commodified
Bridge 4: The institution as carrier of human flourishing

title: Magnifica Humanitas — Chapter 4: Truth, Work, Freedom
publisher: Dicastery for Promoting Integral Human Development
summary: Three interlocked goods that any humane technological order must protect and extend: truth (against the manufactured falsehoods of synthetic media and algorithmic distortion), work (as participation in creation and source of dignity, not as a residual to be automated away), and freedom (rightly ordered to truth and the common good, not as the unconstrained sovereign choice of liberal theory). The chapter reads these as the test conditions on any AI deployment.
bridges: Bridge 1: Promise as the moral substrate of contract
Bridge 5: The dignity of commercial work
Bridge 6: Limits on what may be commodified

title: Magnifica Humanitas — Chapter 5 and Conclusion: Civilization of Love
publisher: Dicastery for Promoting Integral Human Development
original_filename: Infographic 5 - Magnifica Humanitas - Cap 5 & Conclusion - EN.jpg
summary: The constructive horizon: a civilization of love built on the four pillars introduced earlier, with each vocation (scientists, entrepreneurs, workers, educators, legislators) assigned its own section of the wall in the Nehemiah figure. The conclusion calls every reader back to MH 16's framing and issues the mission to take up the proper section in the rebuilding, refusing both the Babel temptation of total control and the despairing temptation of withdrawal.
bridges: Bridge 4: The institution as carrier of human flourishing
Bridge 5: The dignity of commercial work
Bridge 2: The moral architecture of property
